target.name: (-)-Neocucurbol C
target.motivation: Neocucurbols possess a unique and complex polycyclic skeleton and multiple stereocenters, rendering them challenging targets for total synthesis and offering opportunities for innovations in chemistry. No total synthesis of neocucurbols has been reported prior to this work.

success_criteria.free_text: First asymmetric total synthesis of (-)-neocucurbol C.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond cleavage of isopropenyl group from (+)-nootkatone
key_disconnections.reasoning: To remove the extra isopropenyl group from (+)-nootkatone, which serves as a chiral pool starting material containing the AB ring system and two key stereocenters of the target molecule.
To stitch the E ring (an aromatic precursor) onto the decalin ring derived from (+)-nootkatone.
To break the aromaticity of the E ring precursor and form the key oxa-bridge of the oxabicyclo[2.2.1]heptane system.

key_disconnections.named_strategy: metal-catalyzed hydrogen atom transfer (MHAT)-initiated reductive radical cyclization
key_disconnections.reasoning: To form a strategically important bond and the C9 quaternary center, completing the entire framework of neocucurbols A-D.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The synthesis began with pattern-recognition analysis to identify (+)-nootkatone as a suitable chiral pool starting material. The strategy involved removing an unwanted isopropenyl group, followed by a Suzuki-Miyaura cross-coupling to introduce an aromatic E-ring precursor. An oxidative dearomatization cyclization was planned to form the oxa-bridge, and a challenging MHAT-initiated reductive radical cyclization was envisioned to construct the C9-C10 bond and the C9 quaternary center, thereby completing the core polycyclic framework. Peripheral decorations would then complete the synthesis. Model studies were conducted to address concerns regarding stereochemical control in the oxidative dearomatization and the feasibility of the MHAT cyclization.

thought.background: The neocucurbols possess a complex 6/6/5/5/6 polycyclic skeleton and multiple stereocenters, making them challenging synthetic targets. (+)-Nootkatone contains the AB ring system and two key stereocenters of the target molecule.
thought.gap: How to efficiently construct the complex polycyclic framework of neocucurbols from a simpler starting material like (+)-nootkatone.
thought.decision: To utilize pattern-recognition analysis to map (+)-nootkatone onto the AB ring system of neocucurbols. This would require a selective C-C bond cleavage to remove the isopropenyl group, followed by a Suzuki-Miyaura cross coupling to introduce an aromatic E ring precursor, an oxidative dearomatization cyclization to form the oxa-bridge, and a metal-catalyzed hydrogen atom transfer (MHAT)-initiated reductive radical cyclization to form the C9-C10 bond and the C9 quaternary center.

outcome.observation: A comprehensive retrosynthetic plan was developed.

evidence.text_span: Using pattern-recognition analysis,6 we mapped (+) -nootkatone (13) on the AB ring system of neocucurbol A−D (Figure 1C). This would require a selective C-C bond cleavage to amputate the isopropenyl group of (+) -nootkatone (13 15 ). Such surgical removal of the isopropenyl group was recently developed by Kwon and co-workers and has significantly expanded the application of chiral pool molecules.7 With this in mind and using bond-network analysis,8 we decided to stitch the E ring onto the decalin ring derived from (+) -nootkatone by constructing the oxabicyclo[2.2.1]heptane, the most congested ring system of neocucurbols A−D. We further envisioned a substituted benzene (cf. 16) as the E ring precursor, which would allow a cross coupling such as the Suzuki−Miyaura reaction9 to form the C2−C15 bond and link the aromatic moiety to the decalin system (15 17 ) ). A subsequent oxidative dearomatization10 was planned to break the aromaticity and form the C15−O bond (17 18 ) ). How to form the C9−C10 bond became the most challenging step. We wondered about the possibility of using a reductive olefin coupling via a metal-catalyzed hydrogen atom transfer (MHAT)-initiated radical cyclization11 to form this strategically important bond and the C9 quaternary center, delivering the entire framework of neocucurbols A−D 18 19 _ . ). From here on, several peripheral manipulations would be needed to complete the total synthesis. With this said, we were aware of several potential issues associated with this synthetic plan. One major issue is the stereochemical control of the spirocenter formed in the oxidative dearomatization cyclization step. There is no convincing reason that one stereoisomer would be significantly favored over the other one. Second, while there are ample precedents for MHAT cyclization at the β-position of α,β-unsaturated enones, cyclization at the δ-position to form a bridged ring system poses a great synthetic challenge.

thought.background: The retrosynthetic plan identified oxidative dearomatization cyclization and MHAT cyclization as key, but potentially problematic, steps.
thought.gap: The feasibility and stereoselectivity of the oxidative dearomatization cyclization and the MHAT cyclization needed to be evaluated.
thought.decision: To carry out a model study using compound 20 to evaluate the two key cyclization steps.

outcome.observation: Oxidative dearomatization cyclization occurred, but with low diastereoselectivity (d.r. = 2.5/1) and an undesired iodophenyl group addition at the γ-position. The desired product was not observed.

evidence.text_span: With the above concerns, we decided to carry out a model study (Scheme 1A) and prepared 20 (see Supporting Information) to evaluate the two key cyclization steps. When 20 was treated with PIDA in HFIP,12 we did not observe any desired product. Instead, compound 21 and its epimer were produced in 73% yield (d.r. = 2.5/1), slightly favoring 21 presumably due to the directing effect of the tertiary alcohol. While the oxidative dearomatization cyclization did occur, the diastereoselectivity was low. What made it worse is that an iodophenyl derived from PIDA was added at the γ-position.13 Various reaction conditions and hypervalent iodine reagents were investigated to suppress the formation of these byproducts but without any success.

thought.background: The oxidative dearomatization of model compound 20 yielded an undesired iodophenyl adduct with low diastereoselectivity. The subsequent MHAT cyclization was planned to be tested on this product.
thought.gap: The feasibility of the MHAT cyclization on the product from the previous oxidative dearomatization attempt needed to be assessed.
thought.decision: To use the product 21 from the previous step as a probe molecule to test the proposed MHAT cyclization.

outcome.observation: Endeavors to perform the MHAT cyclization on compound 21 were not fruitful.

evidence.text_span: While we could not prevent the formation of 21, we decided to use it as a probe molecule to test the proposed MHAT cyclization. Again, our endeavors were not fruitful.

thought.background: The initial model study for oxidative dearomatization and MHAT cyclization failed due to undesired iodophenyl addition and low diastereoselectivity in the former, and no reaction in the latter. The ortho-hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring was identified as a potential issue.
thought.gap: A new strategy was needed to achieve successful oxidative dearomatization and MHAT cyclization.
thought.decision: To move the hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring from the ortho position to the para position. This maneuver was expected to avoid iodophenyl addition, simplify diastereoselectivity, and make the β-position of the cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-one product more reactive for MHAT cyclization.

outcome.observation: A revised strategy was formulated for the model study.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, we decided to move the hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring from the ortho position to the para position. While such a maneuver would increase the steps for late-stage E ring functionalization, it could avoid the addition of the iodophenyl group and simplify the diastereoselectivity issue. The β-position of the oxidative dearomatization product (a cyclohexa-2,5-dien-1-one) would also be more reactive than the δ-position of the corresponding cyclohexa-2,4-dien-1-one for the MHAT cyclization. Model 23 was then prepared to test this new strategy.

thought.background: A revised model strategy involved moving the hydroxyl group on the aromatic ring to the para position to improve oxidative dearomatization and MHAT cyclization.
thought.gap: The success of the revised oxidative dearomatization on model compound 23 needed to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To treat model compound 23 with PIDA in HFIP to produce the desired product 24.

outcome.observation: The desired product 24 was produced in 60% yield, indicating success for the oxidative dearomatization with the revised strategy.

evidence.text_span: Model 23 was then prepared to test this new strategy. When 23 was treated with PIDA in HFIP, the desired product 24 was produced in 60% yield.

thought.background: The revised oxidative dearomatization on model compound 23 was successful, yielding 24.
thought.gap: The feasibility of the MHAT cyclization on the product 24 from the revised oxidative dearomatization needed to be confirmed.
thought.decision: To subject compound 24 to MHAT cyclization conditions using Fe(acac)3 and PhSiH3 to produce 25.

outcome.observation: Compound 23 underwent MHAT cyclization to give 25 in 75% yield, confirming the success of the revised strategy for both key cyclization steps.

evidence.text_span: Furthermore, 23 underwent MHAT cyclization to give 25 (CCDC 2450485) in 75% yield with the combination of Fe(acac)3 and PhSiH3.14

thought.background: The model studies confirmed the viability of the oxidative dearomatization and MHAT cyclization strategies. The total synthesis can now commence from (+)-nootkatone.
thought.gap: The first step in the total synthesis is to remove the extra isopropenyl group from (+)-nootkatone.
thought.decision: To utilize Kwon’s hydrodealkenylative bond fragmentation to remove the isopropenyl group from (+)-nootkatone, delivering compound 26.

outcome.observation: Compound 26 was delivered in 85% yield on a gram scale, successfully removing the isopropenyl group.

evidence.text_span: With a successful model, we started our total synthesis from commercially available (+) -nootkatone (Scheme 2). Kwon’s hydrodealkenylative bond fragmentation was utilized to remove the extra isopropenyl group, delivering 26 in 85% yield on a gram scale.15

thought.background: The next step is to introduce a hydroxyl group at the γ-position of the enone.
thought.gap: Yue's photoredox C-H hydroxylation was considered, but its scalability and potential for overoxidation were concerns.
thought.decision: To attempt Yue's photoredox C-H hydroxylation to introduce the hydroxyl group in one step.

outcome.observation: Could not scale up this synthesis to gram scale, and the reaction was complicated by overoxidation.

evidence.text_span: We then investigated different protocols to introduce a hydroxyl group at the γ-position of the enone. While Yue’s photoredox C-H hydroxylation was able to introduce the hydroxyl group in one step,16 we could not scale up this synthesis to gram scale and the reaction was complicated by overoxidation (see the Supporting Information).

thought.background: Yue's photoredox C-H hydroxylation for introducing a hydroxyl group at the γ-position of the enone failed to scale up and suffered from overoxidation.
thought.gap: A more robust and scalable method was needed to introduce the hydroxyl group.
thought.decision: To opt for a well-established two-step procedure: extended silyl enol ether formation and m-CPBA oxidation, to produce 27 and its epimer.

outcome.observation: The two-step procedure successfully produced 27 and its epimer in 81% yield (d.r. = 4.4/1) on a gram scale.

evidence.text_span: Thus, we opted for a well-established two-step procedure: extended silyl enol ether formation and m-CPBA oxidation,17 which produced 27 and its epimer in 81% yield (d.r. = 4.4/1 ) on a gram scale.

thought.background: The next step is to introduce an iodide at the α-position of the enone for subsequent cross-coupling. The angular all-carbon quaternary center presents steric hindrance.
thought.gap: Johnson iodination protocol was considered, but its effectiveness in the presence of steric hindrance was uncertain.
thought.decision: To attempt the Johnson iodination protocol (I2, pyridine) for α-iodination.

outcome.observation: Initial attempts with the Johnson iodination protocol were unsuccessful due to steric interference generated by the angular all-carbon quaternary center.

evidence.text_span: At this stage, we decided to introduce an iodide at the α-position of the enone for subsequent cross-coupling to append the aromatic moiety. This α-iodination turned out to be difficult due to the steric interference generated by the angular all-carbon quaternary center. Our initial attempts with the Johnson iodination protocol ΔI2, pyridine) were unsuccessful.18

thought.background: The Johnson iodination protocol failed for α-iodination due to steric hindrance.
thought.gap: A new iodination method was needed that could overcome the steric interference and also protect the secondary alcohol.
thought.decision: To use a combination of TMSN3 and iodine, where TMSN3 functions as a Lewis acid and the azide is a relatively small nucleophile for conjugate addition. Under these conditions, the secondary alcohol would also be protected as a TMS ether, yielding 28 and 29.

outcome.observation: The desired product 28 was obtained in a modest 30% yield, along with 29 (65% yield), which could be recycled. This method successfully circumvented the steric hindrance issue.

evidence.text_span: We were able to circumvent this problem with a combination of TMSN3 and iodine.19 In this case, TMSN3 3 functions as a Lewis acid to activate the enone, and the azide is a relatively small nucleophile for the conjugate addition step. Under these iodination conditions, the secondary alcohol was also protected as TMS ether to give products 28 and 29. While the yield for the desired product 28 was modest (30%) , 29 could be converted back to 27 with TBAF for the next cycle to avoid significant material loss.

thought.background: Compound 28, with the α-iodine and TMS-protected alcohol, is ready for cross-coupling.
thought.gap: The Suzuki-Miyaura cross coupling needs to be performed to append the aromatic moiety (boronic acid 30).
thought.decision: To perform Suzuki-Miyaura cross coupling between 28 and boronic acid 30 using Pd(dppf)Cl2 as catalyst.

outcome.observation: The Suzuki-Miyaura cross coupling occurred smoothly, yielding the coupling product in 87% yield.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent Suzuki−Miyaura cross coupling between 28 and boronic acid 30 occurred smoothly with Pd(dppf)Cl2 as catalyst.20 The coupling product was next reduced to 31 with a Luche reduction.

thought.background: The Suzuki-Miyaura coupling product has been obtained.
thought.gap: The ketone needs to be reduced, and the secondary alcohol needs to be protected as an acetate, followed by TMS group removal, to prepare for directed epoxidation.
thought.decision: To reduce the coupling product to 31 using Luche reduction, followed by a one-pot acetate protection and TMS group removal to convert 31 to 32.

outcome.observation: Compound 31 was converted to 32 in 80% yield after Luche reduction, acetate protection, and TMS group removal.

evidence.text_span: The coupling product was next reduced to 31 with a Luche reduction. After a one-pot acetate protection and TMS group removal, 31 was then converted to 32 for a directed epoxidation with VO(acac)2 and TBHP.

thought.background: Compound 32 is prepared for directed epoxidation.
thought.gap: A fully substituted epoxide needs to be formed.
thought.decision: To perform directed epoxidation of 32 using VO(acac)2 and TBHP.

outcome.observation: The directed epoxidation successfully yielded 33 in 77% yield. The resulting fully substituted epoxide was surprisingly stable.

evidence.text_span: After a one-pot acetate protection and TMS group removal, 31 was then converted to 32 for a directed epoxidation with VO(acac)2 and TBHP. Notably, the resulting fully substituted epoxide is surprisingly stable and survived the following steps until the desired reductive opening.

thought.background: Compound 33, containing the stable epoxide, is ready for further transformation.
thought.gap: The secondary alcohol needs to be oxidized to a ketone, and an α-methylene group needs to be introduced, followed by stereoselective reduction.
thought.decision: To oxidize the secondary alcohol of 33 using Dess-Martin periodinane (DMP), then introduce the α-methylene group using N,N,N',N'-tetramethanediamine as a mild Mannich reagent, and finally stereoselectively reduce the ketone in the same pot to furnish 34.

outcome.observation: DMP oxidation, α-methylene introduction, and stereoselective reduction successfully furnished 34 in 80% yield.

evidence.text_span: Dess-Martin periodinane (DMP) oxidation of the secondary alcohol gave ketone 33. The next step was to introduce the desired α-methylene group, which was accomplished using N,N,N',N'-tetramethanediamine as a mild Mannich reagent.21 The ketone was then stereoselectively reduced in the same pot to furnish 34 in 80% yield.

thought.background: Compound 34 is ready for the oxidative dearomatization cyclization, but first, the benzyl protecting group needs to be removed.
thought.gap: The free phenol product is known to be unstable, requiring immediate progression to the next step.
thought.decision: To remove the benzyl group from 34 using BBr3, and then immediately subject the crude phenol to oxidative dearomatization cyclization with PIDA in HFIP to produce 35.

outcome.observation: Compound 35 was produced in 68% yield from 34, successfully completing the oxidative dearomatization cyclization after benzyl removal.

evidence.text_span: Subsequent benzyl removal with BBr3 set the stage for the oxidative dearomatization cyclization. Notably, the free phenol product is unstable, presumably due to para quinone methide formation via epoxide opening, and needs to be subjected to the next step immediately. Upon treatment of the crude phenol with PIDA in HFIP, the desired product 35 was produced in 68% yield from 34.

thought.background: Compound 35, the product of oxidative dearomatization, is ready for the final cyclization to form the core skeleton.
thought.gap: The reductive radical cyclization (MHAT cyclization) needs to be performed to complete the neocucurbol skeleton.
thought.decision: To treat compound 35 with Fe(acac)3 and PhSiH3 to initiate the reductive radical cyclization, producing 36.

outcome.observation: Compound 36, with the entire neocucurbol skeleton, was produced in 75% yield, successfully completing the core framework.

evidence.text_span: Compound 35 was then treated with Fe(acac)3 and PhSiH3 to initiate the reductive radical cyclization, which produced 36 (CCDC 2424690) with the entire neocucurbol skeleton in 75% yield.

thought.background: The entire neocucurbol skeleton (36) has been established.
thought.gap: Peripheral decoration is needed, specifically the installation of the C13 methyl group.
thought.decision: To install the C13 methyl group via α-iododination followed by Stille cross coupling, yielding 37.

outcome.observation: The two-step process successfully afforded 37 in 70% yield, installing the C13 methyl group.

evidence.text_span: Installation of the C13 methyl group was accomplished by α-iododination followed by Stille cross coupling.22 This two-step process afforded 37 in 70% yield.

thought.background: Compound 37 has the C13 methyl group installed.
thought.gap: The oxygen functionality needs to be migrated from C12 to C14, which is a nontrivial transposition.
thought.decision: To reduce the ketone group of 37 to a secondary alcohol to direct subsequent m-CPBA epoxidation, giving 38.

outcome.observation: Compound 38 was obtained in 81% yield after ketone reduction and m-CPBA epoxidation, setting the stage for oxygen migration.

evidence.text_span: We next needed to migrate the oxygen functionality from C12 to C14, an issue resulting from moving the phenol from the ortho to para position to enable a successful oxidative dearomatization and MHAT cyclization. This maneuver turned out to be nontrivial. To achieve this transposition, the ketone group was first reduced to a secondary alcohol to direct subsequent m-CPBA epoxidation to give 38 in 81% yield.

thought.background: Compound 38, an epoxy alcohol, is ready for reductive elimination to achieve oxygen migration.
thought.gap: The titanocene-promoted reductive elimination needs to be performed, but the stereochemistry at C14 might be an issue.
thought.decision: To subject compound 38 to a titanocene-promoted reductive elimination to afford 39.

outcome.observation: Compound 39 was afforded in 80% yield, but with undesired stereochemistry at C14.

evidence.text_span: Compound 38 was then subjected to a titanocene-promoted reductive elimination23 to afford 39 with undesired stereochemistry at C14, which was fixed with a two-step process: IBX oxidation and Luche reduction.

thought.background: Compound 39 has undesired stereochemistry at C14 after titanocene-promoted reductive elimination.
thought.gap: The stereochemistry at C14 needs to be corrected.
thought.decision: To fix the stereochemistry at C14 with a two-step process: IBX oxidation and Luche reduction, yielding 40.

outcome.observation: The two-step process of IBX oxidation and Luche reduction successfully corrected the stereochemistry at C14, yielding 40 in 88% yield.

evidence.text_span: Compound 38 was then subjected to a titanocene-promoted reductive elimination23 to afford 39 with undesired stereochemistry at C14, which was fixed with a two-step process: IBX oxidation and Luche reduction. Notably, the stereochemistries of the epoxide and the secondary alcohol of 38 are important for the titanocene-promoted epoxyl alcohol reductive elimination.

thought.background: Compound 40 has the correct stereochemistry at C14.
thought.gap: The secondary alcohol needs to be protected, the acetate group hydrolyzed, and the resulting alcohol oxidized to a ketone.
thought.decision: To protect the secondary alcohol of 40 as a MOM ether, hydrolyze the acetate group at C4, and oxidize the resulting alcohol to ketone 41 with DMP in the same pot.

outcome.observation: Compound 41 was obtained in 83% yield after MOM protection, acetate hydrolysis, and DMP oxidation.

evidence.text_span: To complete the total synthesis of neocucurbol C, the secondary alcohol of 40 was protected as a MOM ether. In the same pot, the acetate group at C4 was hydrolyzed and the resulting alcohol was oxidized to ketone 41 with DMP.

thought.background: Compound 41 is the penultimate intermediate.
thought.gap: The MOM protecting group needs to be removed, and the epoxide needs to be reductively opened to yield neocucurbol C.
thought.decision: To remove the MOM protecting group with LiBF4, followed by SmI2-mediated reductive epoxide opening to deliver neocucurbol C.

outcome.observation: Neocucurbol C was delivered in 78% yield, completing the total synthesis.

evidence.text_span: After removal of the MOM protecting group with LiBF4, SmI2-mediated reductive epoxide opening delivered neocucurbol C in 78% yield.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield not explicitly stated, but the synthesis was completed in 24 steps.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis was completed in 24 steps.

Total Steps.interpretation: The total synthesis was completed in 24 steps.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Two explicit failed attempts were reported in the model studies and one in the main synthesis.

validation.significance: This work represents the first asymmetric total synthesis of (-)-neocucurbol C, a complex diterpene natural product. It demonstrates the power of pattern recognition in identifying suitable starting materials and highlights the development and successful application of key transformations, including Kwon’s hydrodealkenylative bond fragmentation, Suzuki-Miyaura cross coupling, oxidative dearomatization cyclization, and MHAT-initiated reductive radical cyclization. The synthesis also led to the identification of synthetic intermediates (35 and 36) with promising anticancer activity, suggesting potential for further drug discovery.

source.title: Total Synthesis of (-)-Neocucurbol C Enabled by Pattern Recognition and MHAT Cyclization.
source.authors: Zhong LP, Gudeman C, Zhen J, Wanasinghe OA, Hellmig J, Collins MJE, Bacsa J, Adibekian A, Dai M.
